title: Lecture 6 2024T1
body:
Lecture 10 – Black-Scholes-Merton Model

Hull Chapters 13 & 14  Lecture’s Outline

▪Brief introduction to stochastic processes (from Ch. 13 and focusing on the lecture notes) ▪Markov process ▪Wiener process ▪Generalized Wiener process ▪Ito process

▪Modeling stock prices – Geometric Brownian Motion

▪Black-Scholes-Merton model

Markov Process

▪Markov process ▪A stochastic process for which only the present value of a variable is relevant for predicting the future. The history of the variable is irrelevant.

▪Markov property of stock prices is consistent with the weak form of market efficiency.

Wiener Process

▪A Wiener process is a particular type of Markov stochastic process with mean change of zero and a variance rate of 1.0 per year.

▪A variable z follows a Wiener process if it has the following two properties:

▪Property 1: The change in z during a short period of time  𝒛 =  𝒕 is where  ~ (0,1).

▪Property 2: The value change in z for any two different short intervals of time are independent. This property implies that z follows a Markov process

Generalized Wiener process

▪A generalized Wiener process for a variable 𝒙 is

 𝒙 = 𝒂𝒕 +𝒃𝒛=𝒂𝒕 +𝒃( 𝒕)

where 𝒂 and 𝒃 are constants.

▪𝒙 is normally distributed with mean 𝒂𝒕 and standard deviation 𝒃 𝒕 .

Generalized Wiener process: continuous time

limit ▪In the limit t dt, we can write the generalized Wiener process as

𝒅𝒙 = 𝒂𝒅𝒕 +𝒃𝒅𝒛=𝒂𝒕 +𝒃( 𝒅𝒕)

Is this process a reasonable model of stock prices?

Ito Process

▪An Ito process is a generalised Wiener process where a and b are functions of the value of the underlying variables x and time t:

𝒅𝒙 = 𝒂(𝒙, 𝒕)𝒅𝒕 +𝒃(𝒙, 𝒕)𝒅𝒛

▪𝒅𝒙 is normally distributed with mean 𝒂(𝒙, 𝒕)𝒅𝒕 and standard deviation 𝒃(𝒙, 𝒕)𝒅𝒛.

▪Suppose 𝒙 = 𝑺 (we model the stock price), how can we specify 𝒂(𝒔, 𝒕) and 𝒃(𝒔, 𝒕) to have a reasonable price dynamics? ▪Guaranteed to have non-negative price ▪Reasonable expected and volatility of the returns on the stock…

Process of Stock Prices

• What if we assume the following: 𝒅𝑺 = 𝑺 𝒅𝒕 +𝑺 𝒅𝒛 which can also be rewritten as

=  𝒅𝒕 + 𝒅𝒛 𝑺 Then:

▪ is the return on S over a small time interval 𝒅𝒕 𝑺 ▪  is the expected rate of return over a small time interval 𝒅𝒕 ▪  is the volatility of return over a small time interval 𝒅𝒕 ▪ This is called geometric Brownian motion. ▪ is normally distributed with mean  𝒅𝒕 and volatility 𝒅𝒕 𝑺 ▪ The dynamics =  𝒅𝒕 + 𝒅𝒛 implies that 𝑆𝑇 is lognormally distributed (𝑺>0) 𝑺 with: • Expected value: 𝐸[𝑆𝑇 ]= 𝑆0 𝑒 T 2 2 T  2 T • Variance: 𝑉𝑎𝑟[𝑆𝑇 ]=𝑆0 𝑒 (𝑒 − 1)

Basic knowledge of differential calculation • Suppose we have a function of two variables: 𝑓 𝑥, 𝑡

• We can find both partial derivatives and the total derivative

• The partial derivative is simply the derivative with respect to one of the components

𝑎𝑡 • Example: Let 𝑓 𝑥, 𝑡 = 𝑥𝑒 , then: 𝜕𝑓 Building • 𝜕𝑥 𝑥𝑒 𝑎𝑡 = 𝑒 𝑎𝑡 𝜕𝑓 𝑎𝑡 𝑎𝑡 • block: 𝑥𝑒 = 𝑎𝑥𝑒 𝜕𝑡

Partial and • Similarly, we can integrate from 𝑎 to 𝑏 to obtain: Total 𝑏 𝑑𝑓 𝑏 ′ ‫ 𝑏 𝑓 = 𝑥𝑑 𝑥 𝑓 𝑎׬ = 𝑥𝑑 𝑥𝑑 𝑎׬‬− 𝑓(𝑎) derivatives 𝑑2𝑓 𝑑 𝑑𝑓 • The second derivative: 2 = 𝑑𝑥 𝑑𝑥 𝑑𝑥  Finding the Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation

Assumptions:

▪The stock price follows a geometric Brownian motion. ▪The short selling of securities is permitted. ▪There are no transactions costs or taxes. ▪All securities are perfectly divisible. ▪There are no dividends during the life of the option. ▪There are no arbitrage opportunities. ▪Security trading is continuous. ▪The risk-free rate of interest is constant and the same for all maturities.

Finding the Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation

• The Black-Scholes analysis is similar to no-arbitrage analysis in valuing options when stock price changes are binomial.

• Let’s take a short a position in a derivative 𝑓 and construct the hedging portfolio made of the stock 𝑆

• Our goal is to find the quantity of 𝑆 to hold to make the portfolio riskless.

• If we achieve this goal, then we know the portfolio returns should be equal to 𝑟 otherwise an arbitrage opportunity exists. From there magic will happen 𝑑𝑆 • Our assumption will be that the stock return dynamics is =  𝑑𝑡 + 𝑑𝑧 𝑆

• We will denote the portfolio:  = − f + n ∗ S

• The portfolio value dynamics is then: 𝑑 = − df + n ∗ dS

• We know the dynamics of dS (by assumption) but what about that of df ? We need help from Ito for that.  Finding the Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation: The Ito lemma • Now suppose we have an Ito process (i.e., a stock price movement):

𝑑𝑆 =  𝑆𝑑𝑡 + 𝑆𝑑𝑧

• and we have a function of this process (i.e., a derivative 𝑓). Recall that for a call (𝑓 = 𝐶 ) for instance at maturity 𝐶 𝑆, 𝑇 = max 𝑆𝑇 − 𝐾, 0

• Further, suppose that our function 𝑓 𝑆, 𝑡 is a continuous function that is twice differentiable.

• This function will have a Taylor series expansion (i.e., an ‘approximation’ of the function that utilizes derivatives). This is:

𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2𝑓 2 𝑑𝑓 = 𝑑𝑆 + 𝑑𝑡 + 2 𝑑𝑆 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑡 2 𝜕𝑆

Building block: Ito’s Lemma

• We can then substitute equations into each other

𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2𝑓 𝑑𝑓 =  𝑆𝑑𝑡 + 𝑆𝑑𝑧 + 𝑑𝑡 +  𝑆𝑑𝑡 +  𝑆𝑑𝑧 2 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑡 2 𝜕𝑆 2

• But we should note the following: • 𝑑𝑡 2 = 0: Intuitively at 𝑑𝑡 → 0, 𝑑𝑡 2 → 0 at a faster pace • 𝑑𝑡𝑑𝑧 = 0: For similar reasons to 𝑑𝑡 2 • (𝑑𝑧)2 = 𝑑𝑡: • Treat this as a rule: it is the variance of 𝑑𝑧 • Intuitively, if 𝑑𝑧 = 𝜖 𝑑𝑡, then (𝑑𝑧)2 = 𝜖 2 𝑑𝑡. But 𝜖 is a standard normal random process in our case, with its variance being 𝜖 2 due to the mean being zero. But the variance is one for standard normal. Thus, (𝑑𝑧)2 = 𝑑𝑡

• Therefore, 𝑑𝑆 2 =  𝑆𝑑𝑡 + 𝑆𝑑𝑧 2 =  𝑆 2 𝑑𝑡, and we have

𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2𝑓 𝑑𝑓 =  𝑆𝑑𝑡 + 𝑆𝑑𝑧 + 𝑑𝑡 +  𝑆 2 𝑑𝑡 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑡 2 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑓  𝑆 2 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝑑𝑓 =  𝑆 𝜕𝑆 + 2 𝜕𝑆 + 𝜕𝑡 𝑑𝑡 +  𝑆𝑑𝑧 𝜕𝑆

Finding the Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation • Recall that the change in portfolio value is: 𝑑Π = −𝑑𝑓 + 𝑛 ∗ 𝑑𝑆

𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 • What if we set 𝑛= (i.e., we choose shares). 𝜕𝑠 𝜕𝑠

• Then, 𝜕𝑓 𝑑Π = −𝑑𝑓 + ∗ 𝑑𝑆 𝜕𝑠 𝜕𝑓  𝑆 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝑑Π = − 𝑆 + + 𝑑𝑡 +  𝑆𝑑𝑧 + ∗  𝑆𝑑𝑡 + 𝑆𝑑𝑧 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑠

𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓  𝑆 2 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝑑Π = 𝑆−𝑆 − − 𝑑𝑡 + ∗  𝑆𝑑𝑧 −  𝑆𝑑𝑧 𝜕𝑠 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝑠

 𝑆 2 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝑑Π = − − 𝑑𝑡 2 𝜕𝑆 𝜕𝑡

• This portfolio is risk free (i.e., no term 𝑑𝑧). Therefore, it must grow at the risk-free rate. That is, 𝑑Π = 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 𝑟Π𝑑𝑡 but since  = − f + ∗ S it means 𝑑Π = 𝑟 − f + ∗ S 𝑑𝑡 𝜕𝑠 𝜕𝑠

• We get: 𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2 𝑓 2 2 𝑟𝑓= + 𝑟𝑆 + 𝜎 𝑆 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑆 2

• This is the Black-Scholes-Merton partial differential equation.   Black-Scholes-Merton differential equation, boundary conditions

▪The pricing formula of a given derivative can only be obtained when the equation is solved for given boundary conditions:

▪for a European call option, the boundary condition is 𝑓𝑇 = max(𝑆𝑇 -X, 0) when t = T.

▪for a European put option, the boundary condition is 𝑓𝑇 = max(K- 𝑆𝑇 , 0) when t = T.

▪for a long forward contract, the boundary condition is 𝑓𝑇 = 𝑆𝑇 – K when t = T.

Black-Scholes Pricing Formula

• Prices of call & put options on non-dividend-paying stock are given by c = SN (d1 ) − Xe N (d 2 )

ln( S / X ) + ( r +  / 2)(T − t ) d1 = ,

ln( S / X ) + (r −  / 2)(T − t ) d2 = = d1 −  T − t

• The function N(•) in the B-S pricing formula is the cumulative probability distribution function for a standardized normal distribution.

Risk-neutral valuation

▪Notice that  does not appear in the BSM differential equation:

𝜕𝑓 𝜕𝑓 1 𝜕2 𝑓 2 2 𝑟𝑓= + 𝑟𝑆 + 2 𝜎 𝑆 𝜕𝑡 𝜕𝑆 2 𝜕𝑆

▪That is, all variables pertaining to risk preference are canceled out of the equation.

▪Therefore, the solution to the equation, the price of the derivative, is the same regardless of the risk preferences.

▪This result led to the principle of risk-neutral valuation discovered subsequently of BSM pricing formula.

Risk-neutral valuation 1. Since risk preferences aren’t important, we can assume that investors are risk- neutral, i.e., that 𝜇 = 𝑟. In this case, S satisfies: 𝑑𝑆 = 𝑟𝑆𝑑𝑡 + 𝜎𝑆𝑑z

2. Calculate the expected payoff from the option under the risk-neutral 𝑄 expectation as 𝐸 (𝑓𝑇 )

3. Discount at risk-free rate: 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

▪ For instance, the price of European call can be derived from solving: 𝑐 = 𝐸 𝑄 𝑓𝑇 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇 = 𝐸 𝑄 max(𝑆𝑇 − 𝑋, 0) 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

▪ For instance, the price of European put can be derived from solving: 𝑝 = 𝐸 𝑄 𝑓𝑇 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇 = 𝐸 𝑄 max(𝑋 − 𝑆𝑇 , 0) 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

Risk-neutral valuation

•The solutions are the same that solving the partial differential equation. We get: c = SN (d1 ) − Xe N (d 2 )

where:

ln( S / X ) + ( r +  / 2)(T − t ) d1 = ,

ln( S / X ) + (r −  / 2)(T − t ) d2 = = d1 −  T − t

N(d1) and N(d2)

▪N(d1) is the number of shares needed for writing one call option in order to obtain a risk-free portfolio (hedge ratio, Δ, i.e., delta hedging).

▪N(d1) measures how sensitive of a call option price is to change of stock price. c = N (d1 ) S ▪N(d2) is the risk neutral probability that the call option will be exercised:

Q(𝑆𝑇 >X)

Using the formula

• Value a European call option with a strike price of $100 for a stock that currently sells for $93. • The time to maturity is 77 days (or 77/365 years), the risk-free rate is 4.82% per annum and the volatility of the stock is 32% per annum.

St = $93 c = SN ( d1 ) − Xe − r (T −t ) N ( d 2 ) X = $100 ln(S / X ) + (r +  2 / 2)(T − t )  = 32% d1 = , r = 4.82% ln( S / X ) + ( r −  2 / 2)(T − t ) (T-t) = 77/365 year d2 = d1 −  T − t =

 $93  ln  + (0.0482 + 0.32 * 0.32 / 2 )* 77 / 365 d1 =  $100  = −0.3510 0.32 77 / 365 d 2 = −0.3510 − 0.32 77 / 365 = −0.4950 c = $93N (−0.3510) − $100e −0.0482*77 / 365 N (−0.4950) = $93 * 0.3628 − $100e −0.0482*77 / 365 * 0.3103 c = $3.02

Estimating Volatility from Historical Data

Implied Volatility

▪It is the volatility implied by an option price observed in the market.

▪For example, suppose that the value of a European call on a non-dividendpaying stock is 1.875 when S=$21, X=$20, r=0.1, and T- t = 0.25.

▪Based on the Black-Scholes model, we can find the value of  (implied volatility) using an iterative search procedure.

▪Use more actively traded options to find 

▪Do not use deep-in or out of money options to find 

Black-Scholes Pricing Model for European Options on a Dividend-Paying Stock

• What if the amount and timing of the dividends during the life of a European option can be predicted with certainty…

• Then, the Black-Scholes formula can be used provided that the stock price is reduced by the present value of all the dividends during the life of the option, the discounting being done from the ex-dividend dates at the risk-free rate. c = S N ( d1 ) − Xe * N (d 2 ) p = Xe N (−d 2 ) − S N (−d1 ) *

Homework questions

Consider a European call option on a stock when there are ex-dividend dates in two months and five months.

The dividend on each ex-dividend date is expected to be $0.50. The current stock price is $40, the strike price is $40, the stock price volatility is 30% per annum, the risk-free rate of interest is 9% per annum, and the time to maturity is six months.

• What is the delta of the option?

• What is the risk-neutral probability of ending in the money in six months?

• What is the option price?

Solution

▪ PV(D) = 0.9741

▪ S* = 40-0.9741=39.0259

▪ What is the delta of the option? d1=0.2020, and N(d1)=0.5800

▪ What is the risk-neutral probability of ending in the money in six months? d2=-0.0102, and N(d2)=0.4960

▪ What is the option price? c = 3.67

Pricing for American call options

▪For American call option on non-dividend-paying stock, the value is the same as the value of European call option.

▪For American call option on dividend-paying stock, Black suggests an approximation procedure: ▪Calculate prices of European call options that mature at times T and ti (the time you may consider to exercise the options prior to maturity), respectively ▪Approximate option price as greater of the two.

Early Exercise of American call:

▪For an American call option on the dividend-paying stock, it may be optimal to exercise the option immediately prior to an ex-dividend date ti if

where Di is a dividend and ti is the right before the stock going ex-dividend.

Example

• Consider a six-month American call option, S=40, X=40, r=0.09, D1=D2=0.5, =0.30, t1 occurs after two months, and t2 occurs after five months.

• When might the option be exercised ? • What is the price of option?

Example

• Consider a six-month American call option, S=40, X=40, r=0.09, D1=D2=0.5, =0.30, t1 occurs after two months, and t2 occurs after five months. When might the option be exercised ? What is the price of option?

Because 0.89 > 0.5 and 0.3 <0.5, the option might be exercised right before the second ex-dividend date.

Black’s approximation: ▪ Using B-S, the value of option maturing just before the final ex-dividend date would be 3.52 ▪ the value of option maturing at T is $3.67. ▪ Therefore: the value of the option = $3.67.

Limitations of Black-Scholes Model

• Pricing bias ▪ if stock prices do not follow a Geometric Brownian Motion; ▪ if the interest rate is stochastic; ▪ if volatility is stochastic

▪Alternative Models ▪Merton’s Stochastic Interest Rate Model ▪Constant Elasticity of Variance Model ▪Stochastic Volatility Models

Summary

▪The Black-Scholes differential equation is derived by creating a risk-free portfolio

▪The price of any derivative on a non-dividend paying stock must satisfy the differential equation

▪Since risk premium is not part of the equation, European options can be valued as if investors were risk neutral

▪To value European options on cash dividend paying underlying assets, adjust the beginning price of the asset.

▪Using Black approximation to obtain the value of American call options of a stock with a cash dividend.

Exercise questions • Hull Chapter 14: 14.1 -14.10, 14.13-14.16, 14.21, 14.29
